children.text: Localizaciones típicas → Columna, cadera, muñeca, húmero, pelvis
Mecanismo → Caída desde altura propia o menor
Exclusiones → Cráneo, C. cervical, manos, pies
Implicaciones: ↑ Riesgo de fracturas subsecuentes → Requiere farmacoterapia\N→ No necesaria DXA para iniciar tratamiento (pero útil seguimiento) (2,3)
DENSIDAD MINERAL ÓSEA (DXA) → T-score ≤ -2.5 → Gold standard diagnóstico\N→ Excluir otras causas de DMO baja (4)
Criterios NBHA (EE.UU.): → Probabilidad 10 años fractura osteoporótica mayor ≥20%\N→ Probabilidad 10 años fractura cadera ≥3% (5,6)
Criterios variables según guías: → T-score ≤-2.5 + fractura por fragilidad →\NT-score ≤-3. 0 sin fracturas → Historia de fracturas múltiples/severas (7,8)
Trastorno esquelético generalizado (1,2) → ↓ densidad ósea →\NDeterioro microarquitectura → ↑ fragilidad ósea → ↑ riesgo fracturas
Primaria → ↓ función gonadal asociada al envejecimiento
Cambios altura → DXA c/1-2 años\Ninicio terapia → Marcadores óseos\N3-6 meses post inicio

Osteoporosis is characterized by low bone mass,\Nmicroarchitectural disruption, and increased skeletal\Nfragility. A clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis may be made\Nin the presence of (algorithm 1) [2-4]:
●Fragility fracture, particularly at\Nthe spine, hip, distal radius,\Nhumerus, rib, and pelvis

●T-score ≤-2.5 standard deviations (SDs) at any site based\Nupon bone mineral density (BMD) measurement by\Ndual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA) (see "Screening for\Nosteoporosis in postmenopausal women and men", section on\N'Candidates for BMD testing')

●High fracture risk – As another means for diagnosis of osteoporosis in\Npostmenopausal women, we agree with the National Bone Health Alliance\Nsuggestion that a clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis may be made if there is a\Nclear elevated risk for fracture [3]. In the United States, for example, a clinical\Ndiagnosis of osteoporosis may be made when the FRAX 10-year probability of\Nmajor osteoporotic fracture (hip, clinical spine, proximal humerus, or forearm) is\N≥20 percent or the 10-year probability of hip fracture is ≥3 percent [2,3]. (See\N"Osteoporotic fracture risk assessment", section on 'Fracture risk assessment\Ntool' and "Overview of the management of low bone mass and osteoporosis in\Npostmenopausal women", section on 'Osteoporosis'.)
All postmenopausal women with osteoporosis\Nshould have an evaluation to exclude causes of low\Nbone mass other than age and estrogen deficiency.\N(See 'Evaluation' below.)

Fragility fracture — A clinical diagnosis of osteoporosis may be made in\Nthe presence of a fragility fracture, particularly at the spine, hip, distal\Nradius, humerus, rib, and pelvis, without measurement of BMD. Fragility\Nfractures are those occurring spontaneously or from minor trauma, such\Nas a fall from a standing height or less. Fragility fractures result from\Nmechanical forces that would not ordinarily result in fracture. Reduced\Nbone density is a major risk factor for fragility fractures.

The most common sites of fragility fracture are the spine (vertebral\Ncompression fractures), hip, and distal radius. Fragility fractures also\Noccur at the humerus, rib, and pelvis. Certain skeletal locations, including\Nthe skull, cervical spine, hands, feet, and ankles, are not associated with\Nfragility fractures. Stress fractures are also not considered fragility\Nfractures, as they are due to repetitive injury. (See "Overview of stress\Nfractures".)

Bone mineral density — In the absence of a fragility fracture,\NBMD assessment by DXA is the standard test to diagnose\Nosteoporosis, according to the classification of the World\NHealth Organization (WHO) [2,5].
T-score — The WHO established diagnostic thresholds for\NBMD (by DXA) according to the SD difference between a\Npatient's BMD and that of a young adult reference\Npopulation (T-score) (table 1) [5].
●≤-2.5 SD – A BMD T-score that is 2.5 SD or more below\Nthe young adult mean BMD is defined as osteoporosis,\Nprovided that other causes of low BMD have been ruled\Nout (such as osteomalacia)
●-1 to -2.5 SD – A T-score that is between 1 and\N2.5 SD below the young adult mean is termed\Nlow bone mass (osteopenia)
●≥-1 SD – Normal bone density is defined as a\Nvalue within 1 SD of the mean value in the young\Nadult reference population

Individuals with T-scores of ≤-2.5 have the highest risk of fracture.\NHowever, collectively there are more fractures in patients with a\NT-score between -1 and -2.5 because there are so many more\Npatients in this category [6]. (See "Overview of dual-energy x-ray\Nabsorptiometry".)

Z-score — The Z-score is a comparison of the patient's BMD to an\Nage-matched population. A Z-score of -2 or lower is considered below\Nthe expected range for age [7]. Thus, the presence of Z-score values\Nmore than 2 SD below the mean should prompt careful scrutiny for\Ncoexisting secondary causes (eg, glucocorticoid therapy or alcoholism)\Nthat can contribute to osteoporosis. (See 'Additional evaluation' below.)

Applicability of WHO criteria — The World Health Organization (WHO)\Nthresholds were chosen based upon fracture risk in postmenopausal\NWhite women. Similar diagnostic threshold values for men are less\Nwell defined, although for any given BMD, the age-adjusted fracture\Nrisk is similar in men and women [8]. We agree with the International\NSociety for Clinical Densitometry (ISCD) recommendations for the\Napplication of the WHO classification in clinical practice [7]:
●Postmenopausal women and men ≥50 years –\NThe ISCD advises that the WHO criteria be\Nused in postmenopausal women and in men\Nage 50 years and older.

●Premenopausal women and men <50 years – The ISCD\Nadvises that the WHO criteria not be used in premenopausal\Nwomen or men under age 50 years, because the relationship\Nbetween BMD and fracture risk is not the same in younger\Nwomen and men.

●Children – The WHO classification should not be used in children (male or\Nfemale under age 20 years), and a diagnosis of osteoporosis cannot be\Nmade in a child based on densitometric criteria alone. Z-scores, not\NT-scores, should be used, since it is not appropriate to compare the BMD of\Nsomeone who has not yet achieved peak bone mass with that of an adult\Nwho has. Osteoporosis can be diagnosed in children based on the presence\Nof a vertebral compression fracture, or a Z-score <-2 in combination with a\Nsignificant fracture history (eg, two long bone fractures before age 10 years\Nor three long bone fractures before 19 years) [9].

Method of BMD measurement — Several different methods are available to\Nmeasure bone mineral density (BMD). DXA is the best available clinical tool\Nfor the diagnosis of osteoporosis and for monitoring changes in BMD over\Ntime. Detailed information about DXA is found elsewhere (see "Overview of\Ndual-energy x-ray absorptiometry"). Other methods of measuring BMD for\Nfracture risk assessment are reviewed separately. (See "Osteoporotic fracture\Nrisk assessment", section on 'Methods of measurement of BMD'.)

Site of measurement — In women who are candidates for BMD testing, we\Nsuggest DXA measurements of the spine and hip because fractures at these\Nsites have the greatest impact on patients' health. Measurement of hip BMD\Nalso has the highest predictive value for hip fracture. In addition, if\Npharmacologic therapy is planned, measurement of spine BMD is useful as it\Nshows less variability and can detect responses to therapy earlier than hip\NBMD. We make the diagnosis according to the lowest T-score measured.

Interference from osteophytes and vascular calcifications on\Nthe spine measurement are common in aging women and\Ninterfere with the assessment of BMD at this site. In this\Nsetting, measurement of hip BMD alone or hip and one-third\Ndistal radius site is sufficient.

In all studies, the risk for most fractures is inversely proportional to bone density\N[10-18]. Some studies suggest that the risk for fracture at a particular site is best\Nestimated by measuring bone density at that site [19-22]. Other studies,\Nhowever, have found that measurement of BMD at any site predicts fracture risk\Nat all sites equally well [23]. The preferred site for measurement of bone density\Nand the number of sites to measure are debatable, and it may vary according to\Nthe clinical situation. The Fracture Risk Assessment Tool (FRAX) can improve\Nthe prediction of fractures above that achieved by BMD. (See "Osteoporotic\Nfracture risk assessment", section on 'Fracture risk assessment tool' and\N"Screening for osteoporosis in postmenopausal women and men", section on\N'Skeletal site to measure'.)
FRACTURA POR FRAGILIDAD\N→ Espontánea o trauma menor\N→ Sitios típicos:
Diagnóstico: fractura vertebral o Z-score <-2\N+ historia fracturas significativa

Decreased bone mass can occur because peak bone mass is low,\Nbone resorption is excessive, or bone formation during remodeling is\Ndecreased. All three processes are likely to contribute, in varying\Ndegrees, to osteoporosis in individual patients. Most postmenopausal\Nwomen with osteoporosis, however, have either age- or estrogen\Ndeficiency-related bone loss due primarily to excessive bone\Nresorption.

Other causes of reduced bone mineral density (BMD) include\Nosteomalacia, malignancy (eg, multiple myeloma), Paget disease of\Nbone, significant weight loss, reduced skeletal loading such as occurs\Nwith spinal cord injury, tobacco use and/or excessive alcohol intake,\Nhyperthyroidism, and hyperparathyroidism. All these causes of reduced\NBMD increase the risk for fracture, and most can be distinguished from\Nestrogen deficiency-related osteoporosis by the clinical history, physical\Nexamination, and laboratory testing. (See 'Evaluation' below.)

Physical abuse should always be considered a possibility in a patient\Nwith fractures, particularly when the fracture burden exceeds what one\Nwould expect based on BMD, or when location of fractures is atypical.\N(See "Elder abuse, self-neglect, and related phenomena", section on\N'Warning signs'.)

Low BMD may also be seen in conjunction with renal osteodystrophy in patients\Nwith decreased kidney function [24-26]. It is difficult to distinguish low bone\Ndensity due to osteoporosis from low bone density due to any of the components\Nof chronic kidney disease-metabolic bone disease. The distinction may be made\Nbiochemically in most cases, but may sometimes require bone biopsy. The\Ndiagnosis, evaluation, and management of osteoporosis in patients with chronic\Nkidney disease and the diagnosis and management of renal osteodystrophy and\Nsecondary hyperparathyroidism are discussed in detail elsewhere. (See\N"Osteoporosis in patients with chronic kidney disease: Diagnosis and evaluation"\Nand "Evaluation of renal osteodystrophy" and "Management of secondary\Nhyperparathyroidism in adult patients with nondialysis chronic kidney disease"\Nand "Management of secondary hyperparathyroidism in adult patients on\Ndialysis".)
INCREMENTO RESORCIÓN ÓSEA →\NDeficiencia estrógenos → Envejecimiento →\NHipertiroidismo → Hiperparatiroidismo
DISMINUCIÓN FORMACIÓN ÓSEA →\NEnvejecimiento → Diabetes → Déficit\Nhormona crecimiento
Neoplásicas → Mieloma\Nmúltiple → Metástasis óseas\N→ Enfermedad de Paget
Mecánicas/Estructurales → Pérdida peso\Nsignificativa → Lesión medular (↓carga\Nesquelética) → Inmovilización prolongada
Maltrato Físico (especialmente si) →\NFracturas > esperado según DMO →\NLocalización atípica fracturas

The goal of evaluation is to exclude causes of low bone mass\Nother than age and estrogen deficiency, such as osteomalacia,\Nhyperthyroidism, and hyperparathyroidism, and to detect\Npotentially remediable causes or other contributing factors to\Nosteoporosis (table 2) [27-31].

Initial evaluation — The initial evaluation includes a history to\Nassess for clinical risk factors for fracture and to evaluate for\Nother conditions that contribute to bone loss, a physical\Nexamination, and basic laboratory tests.

●History and physical examination – Most of the conditions causing\Nosteoporosis can be excluded with a careful history and physical\Nexamination (table 2). Lifestyle factors that contribute to bone loss,\Nincluding smoking, excessive alcohol, physical inactivity, and poor\Nnutrition, should be addressed. Height and weight should be\Nmeasured.

A history of a fragility fracture is an important risk factor for a subsequent fracture\N[10,32,33]. In women who have a vertebral fracture, approximately 19 percent\Nwill have another fracture in the next year [32]. A meta-analysis of 11 cohorts (15,\N259 men and 44,902 women) showed that a previous fracture was associated\Nwith an increased risk of any fracture compared with those without a prior\Nfracture (relative risk [RR] 1.86, 95% CI 1.75-1.98) [34]. Thus, individuals with a\Nhistory of a fragility fracture are a high-risk group that requires evaluation and\Ntreatment. (See "Osteoporotic fracture risk assessment" and "Overview of the\Nmanagement of low bone mass and osteoporosis in postmenopausal women"\Nand "Osteoporotic thoracolumbar vertebral compression fractures: Clinical\Nmanifestations and treatment".)
●Laboratory evaluation – We suggest that\Npostmenopausal women with low BMD (T-score\Nbelow -2.5) and/or fragility fracture have the following\Nbasic tests (table 3) [27,28,31,35]:
•Biochemistry profile (especially calcium,\Nphosphorous, albumin, total protein, creatinine,\Nalkaline phosphatase, electrolytes)

•If the diagnosis of osteoporosis is based upon the presence of\Na fragility fracture, we also obtain a BMD measurement\N(dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry [DXA]), performed on a\Nnonurgent basis, for quantitative assessment of BMD and to\Nmonitor response to therapy

Additional evaluation — The need for additional laboratory evaluation\Ndepends upon the initial evaluation and Z-score (table 3). Women who have\Nabnormalities on initial laboratory testing, suspicious findings on history and\Nphysical examination suggesting a secondary cause of osteoporosis, or\NZ-scores ≤-2 may require additional evaluation to detect these secondary\Ncauses (table 2).

●24-hour urine collection for calcium and creatinine measurement is useful to\Nassess for adequate calcium intake and absorption in women with\Ngastrointestinal disorders, such as inflammatory bowel disease and celiac\Ndisease, or after gastrointestinal surgery, such as gastrectomy or bariatric\Nsurgery. Assessment of urinary calcium is also necessary in women with\Nkidney stones and may be helpful in women with osteoporosis and no risk\Nfactors beyond age to identify idiopathic hypercalciuria [36]. (See "Metabolic\Nbone disease in inflammatory bowel disease" and "Kidney stones in adults:\NEpidemiology and risk factors", section on 'High urine calcium'.)

●A woman with unexplained anemia, vitamin D deficiency, and/or low\Nurinary calcium excretion should be tested for celiac disease. (See\N"Vitamin D deficiency in adults: Definition, clinical manifestations, and\Ntreatment", section on 'Defining vitamin D sufficiency' and "Diagnosis of\Nceliac disease in adults" and "Epidemiology, pathogenesis, and clinical\Nmanifestations of celiac disease in adults", section on 'Metabolic bone\Ndisorders'.)

●Cancer or multiple myeloma should be considered in patients who\Nalso have hypercalcemia, otherwise unexplained anemia, weight loss,\Nor proteinuria. Measurement of serum and urine protein\Nelectrophoresis including free light chains would be indicated in this\Ncase. (See "Multiple myeloma: Clinical features, laboratory\Nmanifestations, and diagnosis".)

●Serum parathyroid hormone (PTH) should be\Nmeasured in patients with hypercalcemia,\Nhypercalciuria, a history of kidney stones, or\Nosteopenia. (See "Primary hyperparathyroidism:\NDiagnosis, differential diagnosis, and evaluation".)

●Urinary cortisol excretion should be measured if Cushing syndrome is\Nsuspected and in patients with unexplained osteoporosis and vertebral fracture\Nsince patients with subclinical hypercortisolism (mild autonomous cortisol\Nsecretion [MACS] without clinical manifestations of Cushing syndrome) are\Nalso at risk for low BMD and fractures. (See "Evaluation and management of\Nthe adrenal incidentaloma", section on 'Subclinical Cushing syndrome' and\N"Establishing the diagnosis of Cushing syndrome" and "Epidemiology and\Nclinical manifestations of Cushing syndrome", section on 'Bone loss'.)

Bone turnover markers — We do not routinely measure markers of bone turnover\N(BTMs) in postmenopausal women with osteoporosis. While the use of BTMs in\Nclinical trials has been helpful in understanding the mechanism of action of\Ntherapeutic agents, their role in the care of individual patients is not well\Nestablished. Potential roles of BTMs in clinical practice include prediction of\Nfracture risk, monitoring response to therapy, and improving compliance with\Ntherapy. Biologic and laboratory variability in BTM values have confounded their\Nwidespread use in clinical practice. This topic is reviewed separately. (See "Use\Nof biochemical markers of bone turnover in osteoporosis".)
